title: RISE Final Round
summary: Second place at the RISE Final Round after a stronger final with harder AI tasks.
body: RISE was one of the competition results that gave me a lot of confidence. The final round was harder than the qualifier and forced me to stay calm while solving AI-style tasks under pressure.
This work entry is here because I want the Work section to track outcomes and the process behind them, not only the final placement.

title: ROAI Selection Camp Qualifier
summary: A high-pressure qualifier round that pushed my practical ML and contest strategy.
body: The ROAI Selection Camp Qualifier was useful because it exposed weaknesses in how I manage experiments and time. It was not only about knowing ML, but also about choosing the right experiment quickly.
I keep this in Work because it shaped how I think about AI competitions: practical decisions matter as much as the model itself.

title: Skillab Innovation Challenge
summary: First place with a project around skills, labour-market analytics, and AI-assisted insight.
body: Skillab was the hackathon connected to Cluj IT Cluster where my team built Skillab Radar, a project around skills, labour-market analytics, and AI-assisted insight.
The challenge was more product-oriented than most contests I join. We had to connect AI, data, and a useful idea in a way that made sense outside a pure coding contest.
The repo is linked here because this is one of the projects where the implementation matters as much as the presentation.

title: NitroNLP Hackathon
summary: Third place with PretrainedLanguageModel in a fast NLP hackathon on Nitro-AI Judge.
body: NitroNLP was a fast team-based NLP competition. It rewarded quick iteration, practical evaluation, and communication inside the team.
This belongs in Work because it was a concrete applied NLP build under contest constraints.

title: CCC School & Data/AI
summary: Placed 4th in School and 12th in Data/AI, mixing algorithmic speed with data thinking.
body: CCC mixed level-based programming with data and AI thinking. I liked it because the structure rewarded persistence: each improvement unlocked more of the task.
It is a good example of the kind of work I enjoy most: problem solving that sits somewhere between algorithms, data, and practical implementation.
